As many people consider it inappropriate Some words ending in -man do not have a common feminine equivalent 5 words     ending      in  -man steward/stewardess.

replacing policeman/policewoman, and flight attendant is replacing manager are now commonly used for women as well as men. Police officer is sexist, and some of the words listed above are going out of use. Actor, hero and The  use of separate terms for women's roles is often felt to be discriminatory or of a male mayor.

A  mayor can be a man or a woman; in Britain a mayoress is the wife manager  manageress      widower widow host     hostess         waiter  waitress hero     heroine        steward stewardess duke     duchess        prince  princess (bride)groombride         policemanpolicewoman actor    actress        monk    nun Man                       Woman                                       Man                   Woman A  few jobs and positions have different words for men and women. OK            Not too bad.        Mustn't grumble. (BrE) Fine/Great, thanks.             All right.              (It) could be worse.

Very well, thank you. And you? Fine, thank you.

How are things? / How's things? (informal)        How (are) you doing?

How are you?                How's it going? (informal) general situation.

When  we  meet people we  know, we  often ask politely about their health or their 3 asking     about     health,     etc together with them.

form G'day), and Goodnight is used only when leaving people, not when getting Note that Good day is very unusual (except in Australian English in its colloquial It was nice to meet / meeting you.

See you. (informal)   See you later / tomorrow / next week, etc (informal) Cheers. (informal - British only)   Take care. (informal) Bye. (informal)       Bye-bye. (often used to and by children) Goodnight.    Goodbye.

When  leaving people: Hello. Hi. (very informal) When getting together with people (informal): (Good) morning/afternoon/evening.

When getting together with people (formal): 2 greetings For the use of first names, surnames and titles, ►    326.

People who  are introduced often shake hands.

Hello/Hi (informal). Americans often say How are you?

When people are introduced, they may say Pleased/Nice to meet you. (formal), or Can/May I introduce Ben Willis? (more formal) I don't think you two know each other, do you?

Sophie, I don't think you've met Laura.

Jack, do you know Ella? Ella, this is my friend Jack.
